name: Statistics for Research
description: The statistics you need to read and write empirical papers with confidence: variance and standard deviation from first principles, standard errors, confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, effect sizes, bootstrap methods, and the ML-specific pitfalls around seeds, benchmarks, and error bars.

fields.front: When should you report the median instead of the mean?
fields.back: When the distribution is skewed or has outliers.
fields.extra: The median tracks the typical case; the mean chases the tail. Latencies, token counts, and per-example losses are usually right-skewed.

fields.front: The mean and median of a dataset disagree a lot. What is that telling you?
fields.back: The distribution is skewed: the mean is being pulled by the tail.
fields.extra: A quick skew check that needs no plot.

fields.front: In words, what does variance measure?
fields.back: The average squared deviation from the mean.
fields.extra: Squaring makes every deviation positive and weights big misses heavily.
The expected squared distance from the mean.

fields.front: Variance is expressed in what units?
fields.back: The data's units *squared*.
fields.extra: Squared units are unreadable, which is why reports use the standard deviation instead.

fields.front: How is standard deviation defined in terms of variance?
fields.back: It is the square root of variance.
fields.extra: sigma = sqrt(Var(X)).

fields.front: Why is standard deviation preferred over variance for reporting?
fields.back: It is in the same units as the data, so it reads as the typical distance from the mean.
fields.extra: 'Accuracy 74 plus or minus 3' only means something because the 3 shares units with the 74.

fields.front: Why does the sample variance divide by n - 1 instead of n?
fields.back: Deviations are measured from the *sample* mean, which sits closer to the data than the true mean does, so dividing by n would systematically underestimate variance.
fields.extra: Degrees-of-freedom view: the n deviations sum to zero, so only n - 1 of them are independent.

fields.text: Dividing by n - 1 in the sample variance is called {{c1::Bessel's correction}}.
fields.extra: It removes the bias from measuring deviations against the fitted sample mean.

fields.front: For independent random variables, which add: variances or standard deviations?
fields.back: Variances: Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y).
fields.extra: The rule behind all uncertainty propagation.

fields.front: How do you combine the SDs of two independent error sources?
fields.back: In quadrature: sd = sqrt(sd_1^2 + sd_2^2).
fields.extra: Never by summing the SDs directly; that overstates the combined error.

fields.front: What is the interquartile range (IQR)?
fields.back: The 75th percentile minus the 25th percentile.
fields.extra: The width of the middle half of the data; a robust measure of spread.

fields.front: What is the median absolute deviation (MAD)?
fields.back: The median of the absolute deviations from the median.
fields.extra: A robust alternative to SD: one extreme outlier barely moves it.

fields.front: Why can a single outlier dominate the SD but barely move the IQR?
fields.back: SD squares deviations, so one extreme value contributes enormously; the IQR depends only on percentile positions.
fields.extra: Same reason MAD is robust: medians ignore how extreme the extremes are.

fields.front: Why is the mean a poor summary of a heavy-tailed quantity (latency, spend per user)?
fields.back: Rare extreme values dominate it, so it is unstable across samples and unrepresentative of the typical case.
fields.extra: A mean latency can be double the median.

fields.front: What should you report instead of the mean for a heavy-tailed quantity?
fields.back: Medians and quantiles, with the tail shown explicitly.
fields.extra: For latency: p50 / p90 / p99.

fields.text: In a normal distribution, about {{c1::68}}% of values fall within 1 SD of the mean, {{c2::95}}% within 2 SD, and {{c3::99.7}}% within 3 SD.
fields.extra: The 68-95-99.7 rule. It is a property of the normal distribution; heavy-tailed data puts far more mass beyond 3 SD than this rule suggests.

fields.front: What does a z-score express?
fields.back: How many standard deviations a value lies from the mean.
fields.extra: Puts different scales on one common footing.
Center, then scale.

fields.front: What is the variance of a Bernoulli(p) outcome (e.g., one eval question scored right/wrong)?
fields.extra: p = 0.9 gives 0.09; p = 0.5 gives 0.25.

fields.front: At what p is the variance of a Bernoulli(p) outcome largest?
fields.extra: Why benchmark scores near 50% are the noisiest for a fixed n, and scores near 0% or 100% the most stable.

fields.front: What is a standard error?
fields.back: The standard deviation of a statistic's sampling distribution: how much the estimate would vary if you reran the whole experiment.
fields.extra: Every statistic has its own SE: means, proportions, differences, regression coefficients.

fields.front: SD vs SE: which describes the data and which describes the estimate?
fields.back: SD describes the spread of individual data points; SE describes the uncertainty of an estimated quantity.
fields.extra: The classic error-bar ambiguity: SD bars answer 'how variable are examples?', SE/CI bars answer 'how sure are we about the average?'.

fields.front: Which shrinks as you collect more data: the SD of the data or the SE of the mean?
fields.back: The SE, like 1/sqrt(n). The data's SD is a property of the population and stays put.
fields.extra: More data makes the estimate more precise, not the data less variable.

fields.front: The standard error of a sample mean equals the sample SD divided by what?
fields.extra: SE = s / sqrt(n). Averaging cancels noise, but only at a square-root rate.

fields.front: How much more data do you need to halve a standard error?
fields.back: Four times as much.
fields.extra: SE scales as 1/sqrt(n): 100x more data buys only a 10x tighter estimate.

fields.front: What does the central limit theorem say about the sampling distribution of a mean?
fields.back: It approaches a normal distribution as n grows, almost regardless of the data's own distribution.
fields.extra: Why mean-based CIs work even on non-normal data.

fields.front: What is the practical caveat to the central limit theorem for skewed or heavy-tailed data?
fields.back: Convergence is slow: at small n the sampling distribution is still skewed, and normal-based CIs undercover.
fields.extra: The CLT also requires finite variance in the first place.

fields.front: What is the standard error of an accuracy (a proportion) measured on n questions?
fields.back: sqrt(p(1 - p) / n).
fields.extra: The instant benchmark noise floor.

fields.front: What is the largest possible SE of a proportion measured on n questions?
fields.back: 0.5 / sqrt(n), at p = 0.5.
fields.extra: n = 1000 questions: up to about 1.6 points. n = 100: up to 5 points.

fields.front: In a 95% confidence interval, what exactly happens 95% of the time?
fields.back: Across many repeated experiments, about 95% of intervals built this way contain the true value.
fields.extra: It is a statement about the *procedure's* coverage, not about any single interval.

fields.front: 'There is a 95% probability the true value is in this particular interval' describes which kind of interval?
fields.back: A Bayesian credible interval, not a frequentist confidence interval.
fields.extra: The most common misreading of a CI.

fields.front: A 95% CI for a mean (large n) is the estimate plus or minus how many standard errors?
fields.back: 1.96 (usually rounded to 2).
fields.extra: 1.96 is the z-value leaving 2.5% in each tail of the normal. Full formula: xbar +/- 1.96 * s / sqrt(n).

fields.front: When do you use the t-distribution instead of the normal for a CI?
fields.back: When the SD is estimated from a small sample.
fields.extra: Its heavier tails compensate for the extra uncertainty in the SD estimate.

fields.front: What is the honest 95% CI multiplier with only 3 data points (df = 2)?
fields.back: 4.30, more than double the usual 1.96.
fields.extra: At n = 10 it is 2.26. This is why 3-seed error bars are enormous when computed honestly.

fields.front: Two models' 95% CIs overlap. Can you conclude there is no significant difference?
fields.back: No: overlapping CIs can still hide a significant difference. Build a CI on the *difference* directly.
fields.extra: Non-overlap does imply a significant difference, roughly.

fields.front: What is the standard error of the difference between two *independent* estimates?
fields.back: sqrt(SE_1^2 + SE_2^2): their variances add.
fields.extra: Only for independent estimates; paired measurements get their own treatment.

fields.front: How do you compute the SE of a difference when the measurements are paired (same questions, same seeds)?
fields.back: Compute the per-item differences, then take the SE of those differences.
fields.extra: Usually much smaller than the independent formula, because item difficulty cancels out.

fields.front: Why does the normal-approximation CI fail for accuracies near 0% or 100%?
fields.back: The sampling distribution is skewed at the boundary, and the formula can produce impossible values like 102%.
fields.extra: Comes up whenever a safety eval reports a near-zero failure rate.

fields.front: Which interval should you use for a proportion near 0% or 100%?
fields.back: A Wilson score interval (or the exact Clopper-Pearson interval).
fields.extra: Both behave sensibly at the boundary where the normal approximation breaks.

fields.front: What is a p-value?
fields.back: The probability of data at least as extreme as yours, assuming the null hypothesis is true.
fields.extra: p = 0.03 means: if there were truly no effect, results this strong would happen 3% of the time by chance.

fields.front: A p-value of 0.03 is NOT the probability of what?
fields.back: That the null hypothesis is true.
fields.extra: The p-value is computed *assuming* the null is true, so it cannot tell you the probability that it is.

fields.front: What is a Type I error?
fields.back: Rejecting a true null hypothesis: a false positive.
fields.extra: Its rate is alpha, conventionally 0.05. Mnemonic: Type I cries wolf.

fields.front: What is a Type II error?
fields.back: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis: a false negative.
fields.extra: Its rate is beta. Mnemonic: Type II sleeps through the wolf.

fields.text: The Type I error rate is denoted {{c1::alpha}}; the Type II error rate is denoted {{c2::beta}}.
fields.extra: Alpha is what you set (0.05 by convention); beta depends on your sample size and effect size.

fields.front: What is statistical power?
fields.back: The probability of detecting an effect that is really there: 1 - beta.
fields.extra: Driven up by larger samples, larger effects, and lower measurement noise.

fields.front: In an underpowered study, what is systematically wrong with the effects that DO reach significance?
fields.back: They are exaggerated: only lucky overestimates make it over the significance bar (the winner's curse).
fields.extra: A surprising significant result from a small experiment is doubly suspect: unlikely to replicate, and inflated.

fields.front: When should you use a paired test instead of an unpaired one?
fields.back: Whenever the same items are measured under both conditions (same benchmark questions for two models, same prompts before/after).
fields.extra: The single biggest free power upgrade in ML evaluation.

fields.front: Why does pairing shrink standard errors so much?
fields.back: It subtracts out item-difficulty variance, leaving only the between-condition signal.
fields.extra: Analyze per-question deltas, not two independent scores.

fields.front: Which test compares two models scored right/wrong on the same set of questions?
fields.back: McNemar's test.
fields.extra: The paired test for binary outcomes.

fields.front: Which questions does McNemar's test actually use?
fields.back: Only the disagreements, where exactly one model is correct; it asks whether that split departs from 50/50.
fields.extra: Questions both models get right, or both get wrong, carry no information about which is better.

fields.front: You test a do-nothing change against 20 benchmarks at alpha = 0.05. How many significant results do you expect by chance?
fields.back: About one (20 x 0.05).
fields.extra: Also applies to hyperparameter sweeps, checkpoints, and eval subsets: every comparison is another lottery ticket for noise.

fields.front: What is the Bonferroni correction for m comparisons?
fields.back: Test each one at alpha / m.
fields.extra: 20 benchmarks at overall alpha = 0.05: test each at 0.0025.

fields.front: What is the garden of forking paths?
fields.back: Making analysis choices (metric, subset, exclusions, stopping point) after seeing the data, which silently multiplies the comparisons and invalidates p-values, even in good faith.
fields.extra: P-hacking without intent.

fields.front: What is the antidote to the garden of forking paths?
fields.back: Fix the primary metric and analysis before running; label everything else exploratory.
fields.extra: In publishing, this is preregistration.

fields.front: A result is statistically significant. What does that NOT tell you?
fields.back: Whether the effect is large enough to matter.
fields.extra: With enough data, trivially small effects become significant: a 0.2-point gain can be 'significant' at n = 100k and still be worthless.

fields.front: What should you report alongside (or instead of) a p-value?
fields.back: The effect size with a confidence interval.
fields.extra: Readers need the magnitude, not just 'probably not zero'.

fields.front: What is Cohen's d?
fields.back: The difference between two means divided by the pooled standard deviation: an effect size in SD units.
fields.extra: Lets you compare effects across scales: a 2-point gain means something different when the seed-to-seed SD is 0.5 vs 5.

fields.front: What are the conventional small / medium / large values of Cohen's d?
fields.extra: Rough conventions, not laws.

fields.front: What does R-squared measure?
fields.back: The fraction of variance in the outcome explained by the model or predictor.
fields.extra: Correlations always look more impressive than the variance they explain.

fields.front: A correlation of r = 0.5 explains what fraction of the variance?
fields.extra: r^2 = 0.25: only a quarter of the variance is shared.

fields.front: You pick the best checkpoint by validation score and it looks worse on the test set. What statistical effect is this?
fields.back: Regression to the mean: selecting the maximum of noisy measurements picks up positive noise, which does not repeat on remeasurement.
fields.extra: Same mechanism behind 'the best prompt from my sweep stopped working': part of its win was luck.

fields.front: Why is reporting the best of N training runs statistically dishonest?
fields.back: The maximum of N noisy outcomes is biased upward, and the bias grows with N: a lucky run is not a reproducible method.
fields.extra: If best-of-N genuinely is the deployment method, benchmark best-of-N *as a procedure*, with fresh runs.

fields.front: What should you report instead of the best of N runs?
fields.back: The mean with a CI across all runs (or the full distribution), stating N.
fields.extra: A strip plot of every run is more honest than a single starred number.

fields.front: What is Simpson's paradox?
fields.back: A trend that holds within every subgroup can reverse when the groups are pooled, because group sizes differ across conditions.
fields.extra: ML flavor: model A beats B on every benchmark category but loses on the aggregate, because the categories have different sizes in each suite.

fields.front: You sample 10 generations for each of 50 prompts. Why is an SE computed from the 500 data points wrong?
fields.back: Generations from the same prompt are correlated, so there are far fewer than 500 independent observations: the naive SE is too small.
fields.extra: The effective sample size is closer to 50, the number of prompts.

fields.front: What is the fix for standard errors on clustered data (many generations per prompt)?
fields.back: Aggregate per cluster first (one number per prompt), or use a clustered bootstrap.
fields.extra: In a clustered bootstrap you resample prompts, not generations.

fields.front: What is the bootstrap procedure?
fields.back: Resample your data with replacement many times, recompute the statistic on each resample, and use the spread of those values as its sampling distribution.
fields.extra: Use on the order of 1000+ resamples.

fields.front: When do you reach for the bootstrap instead of a formula-based SE?
fields.back: When the statistic has no clean SE formula: win rates, Elo, pass@k, medians, ratios.
fields.extra: Anything you can recompute on a resample, you can bootstrap.

fields.front: What kind of error can the bootstrap NOT fix?
fields.back: Bias in how the data was collected: it only quantifies sampling noise.
fields.extra: Resampling a skewed sample just reproduces the skew.

fields.front: How do you get a 95% CI from bootstrap resamples?
fields.back: Take the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of the statistic across resamples (the percentile method).
fields.extra: For clustered data, resample the clusters (prompts, conversations), not the individual rows.

fields.front: How does a permutation test build the null distribution?
fields.back: Shuffle the group labels many times, recomputing the test statistic on each shuffle.
fields.extra: Almost assumption-free; the go-to when you distrust parametric assumptions.

fields.front: In a permutation test, what is the p-value?
fields.back: The fraction of shuffles with a statistic at least as extreme as the observed one.
fields.extra: The null distribution is built directly from your own data.

fields.front: Why not estimate pass@k by literally drawing k samples and checking for a pass?
fields.back: That estimate is high-variance and biased; the combinatorial estimator uses all n samples you generated.
fields.extra: Same trick applies to any best-of-k metric.

fields.front: With n samples per problem and c correct, what is the unbiased pass@k estimate (Chen et al. 2021)?
fields.back: 1 - C(n-c, k) / C(n, k) per problem, averaged over problems.
fields.extra: C(a, b) is the binomial coefficient.

fields.front: What must a paper state for its error bars to mean anything?
fields.back: What the bars are (SD, SE, or a CI at some level) and what the variation is over (seeds, prompts, questions, resamples).
fields.extra: SD-over-seeds and SE-over-questions can differ by an order of magnitude on the same experiment.

fields.front: Why are error bars computed from 3 seeds close to meaningless?
fields.back: An SD estimated from 3 points is itself extremely noisy, so the interval is both huge and unstable.
fields.extra: And the honest multiplier at df = 2 is 4.30. If seeds are expensive, show all three points; a strip plot is more honest than a bar with tiny whiskers.

fields.front: Model A scores 72%, model B scores 74% on a 500-question benchmark. Is B better?
fields.back: Unclear: the SE of each score is about 2 points, so the gap is about one SE, well within noise for an unpaired comparison.
fields.extra: Settle it with a paired per-question analysis (McNemar).

fields.front: What is the first reflex when someone claims a benchmark gap?
fields.back: Convert the gap into units of standard error.
fields.extra: A gap under about 2 SE is not distinguishable from noise.

fields.front: Roughly how many benchmark questions do you need to reliably detect a 1-point accuracy difference, unpaired?
fields.back: Tens of thousands (about 20k-40k depending on the power target).
fields.extra: Required n scales as 1 over the squared difference. Paired analysis on shared questions cuts this dramatically.

fields.front: What is the quickest variance audit before claiming your method beats the baseline?
fields.back: Rerun the *baseline* twice (new seed) and see how big a 'gain' pure noise produces.
fields.extra: The main noise sources to control: training seed, data order, sampling temperature, eval subset, and judge noise.

fields.front: When should you analyze a quantity in log space?
fields.back: When it is positive and multiplicative in nature: effects are ratios and distributions are right-skewed (latencies, losses, compute, prices).
fields.extra: Scaling-law plots are log-log for exactly this reason: power laws become straight lines.

fields.front: Which average corresponds to analyzing in log space?
fields.back: The geometric mean: the exponential of the mean of the logs.
fields.extra: The right average for ratios and growth rates.

fields.front: Your safety eval shows 0 failures in 100 trials. What can you claim about the true failure rate?
fields.back: Only an upper bound: about 3% at 95% confidence. Zero observed failures never means zero risk.
fields.extra: From the rule of three.

fields.text: Rule of three: with 0 events in n trials, the 95% CI for the event rate is approximately [0, {{c1::3/n}}].
fields.extra: 0 failures in 30 trials: upper bound about 10%.
